title: Sgraffito on thrown stoneware greenware
subtitle: Scratching through a slip coat on a thrown pot to reveal the clay body
excerpt: A black slip is brushed over a leather-hard thrown stoneware bowl or cylinder and allowed to firm up. A sgraffito tool is then used to scratch designs through the slip layer, exposing the pale clay body beneath. The contrast between the dark slip and the pale stoneware body creates the decoration.
sourceNotes: Sgraffito on wheel-thrown stoneware technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A surface decoration technique where a contrasting slip layer is scratched through to reveal the clay body beneath. The word means 'scratched' in Italian.
A clay drying stage where the piece is firm enough to handle and decorate without distorting. Slip is applied at soft leather-hard; sgraffito scratching is done at medium to firm leather-hard.
Liquid clay, usually the same body as the pot but tinted with metal oxides or stains, used to coat and decorate ceramic surfaces before firing.

content.text: on a thrown pot uses the contrast between the
and the clay body as decoration. Apply the slip when the pot is soft
. Scratch the design once the slip has firmed to a firmer leather-hard state: at that point the slip should not smear when touched, but the tool still cuts cleanly without powdering.

attrs.content: Wear an N95 mask when scratching through dried slip. Sgraffito work produces fine ceramic dust. Wipe up debris with a damp cloth — never dry sweep. Fire kilns in a well-ventilated space.

items.name: Wheel-thrown stoneware bowl or cylinder at leather-hard stage
Sgraffito tool or lino-cutter
items.substitution: Dental pick or sharp cocktail stick

content.text: Place the leather-hard thrown piece on the banding wheel. Stir the black slip to a smooth, single-cream consistency.

Apply the slip to the exterior of the pot with the mop brush in broad, even strokes. Spin the banding wheel as you work to reach the full circumference. Apply two coats for even coverage, allowing the first coat to lose its shine before applying the second.

Allow the slip to firm up. The surface should feel dry to the touch but the pot should still feel cool and slightly yielding, not bone-dry. This typically takes 30-60 minutes depending on ambient conditions.

Hold the sgraffito tool like a pencil and draw through the slip surface with consistent pressure. The slip should cut cleanly and the pale clay body should appear below. Vary the tool pressure to vary line width. Curved lines follow the pot form well; for an even repeat pattern, mark guide points with the needle tool first.

Clear the slip shavings by tapping the piece over a bin; they will dry and lift off cleanly at this moisture level. Do not attempt to blow them away; use a soft brush or damp fingertip for any fine residue in carved channels.

Allow the piece to continue drying slowly. Cover loosely if the surface is thick with slip to avoid cracking at the slip-clay boundary. Bisque fire as normal. After bisque firing, the sgraffito lines will be clearly visible as pale lines against the dark slip. Apply a clear or semi-transparent glaze over the surface by brushing for the glaze firing; avoid glaze tong dipping as it can chip the slip coating.
Apply white slip to a darker clay body such as red earthenware for a different contrast direction.
Mix sgraffito and underglaze: scratch through the slip to show the clay, then brush underglaze into the grooves before bisque firing.

items.symptom: Slip peels away from the pot surface during drying
items.cause: Slip applied to pot that was too dry; slip and pot shrinking at different rates
items.fix: Apply slip when the pot is soft leather-hard: cool to the touch but not stiff. If the pot is too dry, lightly dampen the surface before slip application

items.symptom: Sgraffito lines look torn and ragged rather than clean
items.cause: Scratching when the slip is too dry; it powders and cracks rather than cutting
items.fix: Work earlier in the drying process. The slip should cut with a smooth action. If it powders, it is too dry; lightly breathe warm air over the surface to re-dampen slightly before continuing
